source_metadata.title_raw: Robert N. Black, Esq. - A-19-101 - June 11, 2019 - Crescent City

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Robert N. Black, Esq. 
General Counsel 
Crescent City Harbor District 
710 H Street 
Crescent City, CA 95531 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-101 
Dear Mr. Black: 
June 11, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Crescent City Harbor District 
Commissioner Carol White regarding the conflict of interest provisions of Government Code 
Section 1090, et seq .1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest 
provisions of Section 1090 and not under other conflict of interest pro hi bi tions, such as the common 
law. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Del Norte County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097. l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (Section 1097.l(c)(5):) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit the Crescent City Harbor Commission from leasing property to 
the Port O'Pints Brewing Company that Commissioner Carol White co-owns? 
1 Sections 1090 through 1097.5. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-19-101 
Page No. 2 
Yes. Section 1090 prevents the Crescent City Harbor Commission from leasing property to 
the Port O'Pints Brewing Company as long as Commissioner White is a member of the Harbor 
Commission. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
Dr. Carol White has been a Crescent City Harbor Commissioner since January 19, 2019. 
The Harbor Commission is a five-member governing body of the Harbor District, another political 
entity. 
In her private capacity, Dr. White is co-owner, with her husband, of the Port O'Pints 
Brewing Company. Three to four years before Dr. White was elected to the Harbor Commission, 
she and her husband were approached by the former CEO of the Harbor District and asked to 
consider Port O'Pints taking a lease on a vacant building in the Harbor. The timing of such a move 
was wrong for the business at that time. 
Circumstances have changed so that a relocation of Port O'Pints to the Harbor can now be 
considered. No negotiations or terms of a potential lease have been considered by the Harbor 
District or presented to Dr. White or her husband since the initial discussions. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090: 
Section 1090 codifies the common law prohibition against "self-dealing" with respect to 
governmental contracts. Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their 
official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition 
applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson 
v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, 
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering 
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) 
As a general rule, when Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the 
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. Moreover, the statutory 
prohibition cannot be avoided by having the board member with the proscribed financial interest 
abstain from participating in the decision-making process. (Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of 
Del Norte (1970) 68 Cal.App.3d,201, 211-212.) The Legislature, however, has created various 
statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where the interest involved is deemed a "remote 
interest," as defined in Section 1091 or a "noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. 


File No. A-19-101 
Page No. 3 
As co-owner of the Port O'Pints Brewing Company, Commissioner White has a prohibitory 
financial interest in any contract between the brewery and the Harbor Commission unless a 
statutory exception applies. 
Landlord or Tenant of the Contracting Party. 
Section 1091(b)(5) provides that a public official who is "a landlord or tenant of the 
contracting party" has a remote interest in that contract. However, as explained below, the "tenant" 
relationship that Commissioner White has with the Harbor District Commission does not'trigger the 
application of the landlord/tenant exception under Section 1090. Because that exception does not 
apply when the public entity is the landlord and the official is the tenant. 
Under Section 1090 where an official has a financial interest, the "contracting party" is not 
the public entity that the official represents. Instead, that term refers to the party who is attempting 
to contract with the public entity of the financially interested official. (See, e.g., 84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158 (2001) [councilmember had "landlord" relationship with the contracting 
party (architectural firm) who was seeking to contract with city].) 
The conclusion that the landlord/tenant exception under Section 1090 does not apply is 
bolstered by case law. For instance, in City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 
the city sought declaratory relief as to whether the city council could renew or extend the contract it 
had with a beach concession operator, who was also a councilmember. (Id. at p. 193.) The contract 
involved the operation of a concession stand to sell bait, fishing tackle, and refreshments on a 
municipal pier. (Id. at p. 194.) After her election to the city council, the councilmember sought to 
exercise the option to renew the contract, but the city refused because it was prohibited by Section 
1090. (Ibid.) 
The court in Imperial Beach held that the exercise of the option to renew would constitute 
the making of a contract in violation of Section 1090 as long as she was a member of the city 
council. (Id. at p. 197.) In doing so, it emphasized that although the councilmember's integrity was 
above reproach, she would have to decide whether to remain on the city council or as owner of the 
concession. The purpose of Section 1090 is "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to 
strike at the appearance of impropriety." (Ibid.) 
Commissioner White would like to enter into a lease of a building with the Commission to 
relocate her brewery. Forming a lease would both constitute the making of a contract and present 
the appearance of a conflict of interest that Section 1090 prohibits. Unless the Commissioner 
divests the proscribed financial interest or removes herself from the Commission, the Commission 
is prohibited from entering into the lease. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 


RPOC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
File No. A-19-101 
Page No. 4 
By: 
Ryan O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Division

the Port O'Pints Brewing Company as long as Commissioner White is a member of the Harbor 
Commission.
